metadata.title: The Question Nobody Wants to Ask

tags: The Question Nobody Wants to Ask
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Replacing human survey respondents with AI assumes the model knows private human attitudes. Test-retest reliability shows even repeated human surveys of political attitudes are noisy. AI-generated responses correlate with real attitudes at only r squared equals 0.20. Most of the variance in what people truly think remains unexplained by the model.

artifactLines: Replacing human survey respondents with AI assumes the model knows private human attitudes.
Test-retest reliability shows even repeated human surveys of political attitudes are noisy.
AI-generated responses correlate with real attitudes at only r squared equals 0.20.
Most of the variance in what people truly think remains unexplained by the model.

beats.narration_text: The claim that AI respondents don't know what humans privately think, landing at only r squared equals 0.20 against real attitudes, makes me want to check the ceiling honestly. Given that political-attitude test-retest reliability is itself imperfect, help me: (1) explain why r squared of 0.20 means most variance is unexplained; (2) work out how a survey's own reliability caps the best possible AI-human correlation; (3) name one research question where 0.20 is still worth the spend.

beats.narration_text: Written by Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. Humanitarians AI is an educational bridge program where Fellows learn experiential AI by doing AI — building real projects and shipping real research.
